Alright, so I'm trying to figure out which mutant ID is causing all these test failures in the Joda-Time project. The user provided a list of 124 failing tests, each pointing to a specific mutant ID: src/main/java/org/joda/time/chrono/ZonedChronology-109-5. My task is to determine if this mutant is adjacent (i.e., part of a contiguous block of mutants) that might be causing the issues.

First, I need to understand what ZonedChronology does in Joda-Time. From what I recall, ZonedChronology manages time zones and their transitions, including daylight saving time changes. So any bug here could affect date/time calculations across different regions, especially around DST periods.

Looking at the mutant ID: src/main/java/org/joda/time/chrono/ZonedChronology-109-5. The number 109 likely refers to a line in the ZonedChronology.java file where a mutation was introduced. The -5 might indicate that this is the fifth such mutation on that line.

Now, considering the output format required, I need to determine if this mutant is part of a contiguous block. Contiguous mutants would mean that multiple mutations are clustered together in the same area of code, possibly causing related issues. If they're spread out, it's less likely they're adjacent and affecting each other.

Since all failing tests point to the same mutant ID, it suggests that this specific mutation is the culprit. But I should check if there are any nearby mutants (like 108-5 or 110-5) that might be part of the same block. If so, they could be fault-adjacent.

I don't have access to the full source code or the list of all mutants, but based on standard practices, each mutant is usually isolated unless they're in a loop or conditional blocks. Therefore, it's possible that this mutant stands alone and isn't part of an adjacent block causing multiple failures.

So, I'll conclude that this mutant isn't fault-adjacent because there are no other mutants listed with IDs close to 109-5.
</think>
